schools.summary: The LORD speaking concerning the test of the rods: 'and the LORD spake unto Moses, saying' — the LORD speaking to Moses, now to give the sign of the twelve rods, by which the priesthood of Aaron would be vindicated (ch. 17, the speaking, God's command, the new section, the occasion, after the rebellion of Korah and the murmuring of the people against the priesthood, now the sign that would settle whom God had chosen, the budding rod ending the strife, the figure, God himself settling the question of the priesthood by a sign, that the murmuring cease, the truth that God vindicates his own appointments, the choice made plain by the sign, God speaking concerning the rods, showing his care to end the strife and confirm his chosen priest); the LORD speaking to Moses (the sign of the rods) — the new section (the test of the priesthood); the LORD spake unto Moses — the sign of the rods introduced (the providence, God settling the priesthood by a sign) — Henry, Gill, Calvin, Keil.

schools.summary: The twelve rods of the tribes, each named: 'speak unto the children of Israel, and take of every one of them a rod according to the house of their fathers, of all their princes according to the house of their fathers twelve rods: write thou every man's name upon his rod' — a rod taken from each tribe, twelve rods, each prince's name written on his rod (the take... a rod according to the house of their fathers, a rod from each tribe, the twelve rods, one for each tribe, the write thou every man's name upon his rod, each rod named, the figure, the twelve rods, the tribes represented, each named, set before God for the test, the rods, dead dry sticks, that God would make to declare his choice, the figure of the contest brought before God, each name written, the matter left to God's sign, the dead rods awaiting the breath of God, that one might live and so declare the chosen); the twelve rods, one per tribe (the tribes represented) — the rods (the twelve); 'write thou every man's name upon his rod' (each rod named, the matter set before God, the figure, the dead rods awaiting God's sign, the providence, the rods prepared, Aaron's name on Levi's rod to follow, vv.3) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

schools.summary: The budding rod to declare the chosen and end the murmuring: 'and it shall come to pass, that the man's rod, whom I shall choose, shall blossom: and I will make to cease from me the murmurings of the children of Israel, whereby they murmur against you' — the rod of the man God chose would blossom, and so the murmuring would cease (the the man's rod, whom I shall choose, shall blossom, the sign of the choice, the budding rod, the I will make to cease... the murmurings of the children of Israel, the strife ended by the sign, the figure, the budding rod the token of God's choice, the dead rod made to live declaring the chosen, the divine election made plain, that the murmuring against the priesthood cease, the truth that God settles the question of his own appointments, the sign silencing the strife, the figure of the chosen one declared by life out of death, the budding rod the witness); the budding rod the token of the choice (the dead rod made to live) — the sign (the budding declares the chosen); 'I will make to cease... the murmurings of the children of Israel' (the murmuring ended by the sign, the strife silenced, the figure, God settling his own appointment, the providence, the sign appointed, the budding to follow, vv.8) — Calvin, Ellicott, Gill.

schools.summary: Aaron's rod that budded, blossomed, and bore almonds: 'and it came to pass, that on the morrow Moses went into the tabernacle of witness; and, behold, the rod of Aaron for the house of Levi was budded, and brought forth buds, and bloomed blossoms, and yielded almonds' — the budding rod — 'the rod of Aaron... was budded, and brought forth buds, and bloomed blossoms, and yielded almonds': overnight, Aaron's dead, dry rod, alone of the twelve, put forth buds, blossoms, and ripe almonds together, the full token of life (the witnesses noting the significance — the dead rod made to live, the divine choice of Aaron's priesthood declared by the miracle of life, the lesson — the budding rod a figure of the priesthood of Christ, raised from the dead, the dead rod brought to life and fruit a figure of the resurrection, life out of death, the priesthood confirmed not by nature but by the power of God, the almond, the first tree to wake and blossom in the spring, a token of the swift and certain working of God, Jeremiah 1:11-12, the New Testament use — Aaron's rod that budded laid up in the ark, Hebrews 9:4, a memorial of the chosen priesthood, the figure — Christ the true High Priest, chosen of God and raised from the dead, 'made... after the power of an endless life,' Hebrews 7:16, the dead rod that buds a figure of the resurrection and the living priesthood of Christ, who, dead and risen, is the chosen Priest forever, the truth that God's choice is sealed by life out of death, the buds, blossoms, and fruit all at once the full witness of the living priesthood, the figure of the resurrection life of Christ that confirms his priesthood and silences all rivals, the figure of life and fruit out of a dead rod, the sign of God's chosen Priest); Aaron's dead rod budding alone (the divine choice declared by life, Jeremiah 1:11) — the budding (the dead rod made to live); the blossoms and ripe almonds together (the full token of life, the priesthood of Christ raised, Hebrews 7:16; 9:4) — the figure (the resurrection life, the living priesthood, the providence, the rod budded, the rods brought out to follow, vv.9) — Calvin, Ellicott, Henry, Wesley, Gill.

debates.question: What does Aaron's budding rod signify?

sides.position: The dead dry rod made to live and bear fruit, the divine choice of the priesthood sealed by the miracle of life - a figure of the priesthood of Christ raised from the dead, the High Priest made after the power of an endless life (Hebrews 9:4; 7:16), life out of death (Jeremiah 1:11-12)

schools.summary: The rods brought out and each man taking his own: 'and Moses brought out all the rods from before the LORD unto all the children of Israel: and they looked, and took every man his rod' — Moses brought out the rods and showed them, and each man took back his own dead rod, Aaron's alone budded (the Moses brought out all the rods from before the LORD, the rods shown to all, the they looked, and took every man his rod, each taking back his own, the dead rods, the figure, the contrast made plain, the eleven dead rods taken back, Aaron's alone living, the sign seen by all, the choice undeniable, the truth that the difference was manifest, the living rod against the dead, the choice of God clear to every eye, the figure of the one living priesthood among the dead, the chosen plainly declared); the rods shown to all (the sign made public) — the showing (the rods brought out); 'they looked, and took every man his rod' (each taking back his own dead rod, the contrast plain, the figure, the eleven dead against Aaron's living, the providence, the choice manifest, the rod kept to follow, vv.10) — Gill.

schools.summary: The people's despairing cry at the nearness of the holy: 'and the children of Israel spake unto Moses, saying, Behold, we die, we perish, we all perish' — the cry — 'Behold, we die, we perish, we all perish': after the judgment on Korah and the sign of the rod, the people cried out in terror at the danger of coming near the holy things and the holy God (the witnesses noting the significance — the despairing fear of the people, overwhelmed by the judgments, terrified at the nearness of the holy God, feeling that any approach meant death, the lesson — the terror of the sinner before the holiness of God without a mediator, the people, conscious of their sin and the deadly holiness of God, despair of drawing near, the fear that 'who can stand before this holy LORD God?' 1 Samuel 6:20, 'if a beast touch the mountain, it shall be stoned... so terrible was the sight,' Hebrews 12:20-21, the cry of the soul that feels its sin and the consuming holiness of God, the figure — the need of a mediator, the terror of access without one, answered in the very next chapter by the charge of the priests who would bear the iniquity of the sanctuary, ch.18, the figure of the sinner's dread before the holy God, and the comfort of the priesthood that makes a way of access, fulfilled in Christ our great High Priest through whom we 'come boldly unto the throne of grace,' Hebrews 4:16; 10:19-22, the truth that without a mediator the holiness of God is a consuming terror, but through the priest there is a way to draw near, the figure of the dread of the holy and the need of the mediator); the people's despairing cry, terrified at the judgments and the holy (the dread of the holy, 1 Samuel 6:20) — the despair (the cry of terror); 'we all perish' (the fear that all would perish at the nearness of the holy, the dread without a mediator, Hebrews 12:20-21, the figure, the need of the priest who makes access, Hebrews 4:16, the providence, the despair, the question of access to follow, vv.13) — Calvin, Clarke, Wesley, Gill.

debates.question: What does the people's despairing cry reveal?

sides.position: The terror of the sinner before the holiness of God without a mediator, the dread that any approach means death (1 Samuel 6:20; Hebrews 12:20-21) - answered in the priesthood that bears the iniquity of the sanctuary (ch.18) and fulfilled in Christ through whom we come boldly to the throne of grace (Hebrews 4:16; 10:19-22)

schools.summary: The despairing fear that all who draw near must die: 'whosoever cometh any thing near unto the tabernacle of the LORD shall die: shall we be consumed with dying?' — the people, in their terror, feared that any who came near the tabernacle must die, and cried, must we all be consumed?
